title: Mapping a group other than user default with --userns=keepid:uid=<n>,gid=<n>
body:
### Feature request description

If I run a container with --userns=keepid:uid=1000,gid=1000 (together with bind mounts), inside the container UID 1000 is mapped to my uid on the host system, and GID 1000 is mapped to the my gid on the host system.  So, I have access to directories and subdirectories with the right permissions and ownership on the host system that are bind-mounted into the container.

However, the gid that is mapped seems always to be the default gid that's listed in /etc/passwd on the home system.  I want to be able to map a different group.

For example:

On the host system, I have username raknop (UID 5000) and group raknop (GID 5000).  I am also a member of group group2 (GID 5001).

If I run a container with --userns=keepid:uid=1000,gid=1000, and have UID 1000 and GID 1000 *inside* the container, when I create files they have UID 5000 and GID 5000 *on the host system*.

However, I want to be able to do "newgrp group2" followed by running a container with "--userns=keepid:uid=1000,gid=1000", and then have files created have GID 5001 on the host system.  Empirically, however, even if I do "newgrp group2" before running the container, gid 1000 inside the container is still mapped to 5000 outside the container, not 5001 as I'd hoped.

Is there a way to use a group other than the user's default group in /etc/passwd with --userns=keepid?

### Suggest potential solution

Either another option that does what I want that I haven't found, or a change to the behavior of --userns=keepid:uid=<n>,gid=<m> so that <m> gets mapped to the currently active group, not the user's default group.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

_No response_

### Additional context

The goal is to create containers that are able to read and write group-owned bind-mounted directories.  The users who will be using the container are members of the group in question, but that group is not their default group.

comments.body:
are your additional groups also part of the `/etc/subgid` file?

Otherwise it is not possible to map these groups inside the rootless user namespace
I only want to map one group, I just want to map one other than the default group.

Does not matter the amount of groups.  If a group has to be within the user namespace of a rootless user, it needs to be in the /etc/subgroup of that user, so it can be stuck into the user namespace via the newgidmap which is setgid and uses this file to control which groups can be shared by a user.
...but you *can* map one group without that, that's what --userns=keep:uid=...,gid=... does.  What I'm looking for is a way to change which one group gets mapped.

the current group can be mapped automatically, you don't even need newgidmap for that, that is different for the additional groups.

How should `/proc/self/gid_map` look like?

If you want a finer control on the mappings, you can use `--uidmap` and `--gidmap` without using the `keep-id` helper

I've been trying to get this to work with --gidmap, and I can't figure out how to do it.

In the system I'm on, my UID is (say) 5000; in /etc/passwd, my default group is also 5000.  So,
```
% id
uid=5000(rknop) gid=5000(rknop) groups=5000(rknop),group2(5555)
```

I want to map UID 5000 on the host to 1000 in the container, and GID 5555 on the host to 1001 in the container.  I've tried:

```
% newgrp group2
% id
uid=5000(rknop) gid=5555(group2) groups=5000(rknop),group2(5555)
% podman run -it --entrypoint /bin/bash \
      --uidmap 1000:0:1 \
      --uidmap 0:1:1000 \
      --uidmap 1001:1001:$((65536-1001)) \
      --gidmap 1001:0:1 \
      --gidmap 0:1:1001 \
      --gidmap 1002:1002:$((65536-1002)) \
  <image>
```

Inside the image:
```
% cat /proc/self/gid_map
      1001          0          1
         0          1       1001
      1002       1002      64534
```

which is I *think* what I want.  However, if I do anything like create files on a bind-mount, or try to read files, it's clear that GID 1001 inside the container has been mapped to GID 5000 outside the container, not GID 5555.

I haven't been able to figure out how to convince --gidmap to map 5555 outside the container to 1001 inside the container.

group2 *is* listed in `/etc/subgid` -- of course, with a different range from `raknop` in `/etc/subgid` and `/etc/subuid` (which have the same range as each other).

what is the canonical mapping you get if you do `podman unshare cat /proc/self/gid_map`?  Is the new gid present there?

If not, you may need to run `podman system migrate` to recreate the user namespace with all the available mappings.

Then you can look up what ID was assigned to your 5555 on the host.  I realize this is a bit tedious at the moment, fortunately @zeehio is working on a fix for this: https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/18713 so that you'd just use `--gidmap 1000:@5555:1` to get 5555 from the host

@rknop Thanks for creating this issue.  I was going to make one but you beat me to it.

@guillaumerose We discussed this issue a few weeks back.  

The desired behavior would be for podman to retain the group of the executing shell.  So if newgrp is used to switch to a different group for the shell/process then podman would retain that. 

Here is an example using unshare...

```
canon@perlmutter:login26:~> unshare -U --map-group=1000 --map-user=canon
canon@perlmutter:login26:~> cat /proc/self/gid_map
      1000    1016907          1
canon@perlmutter:login26:~> exit
logout
canon@perlmutter:login26:~> newgrp das
canon@perlmutter:login26:~> unshare -U --map-group=1000 --map-user=canon
canon@perlmutter:login26:~> cat /proc/self/gid_map
      1000      65982          1

```

I think this demonstrates that this isn't disallowed by the kernel.

If I try this with Podman, I will always get the default group associated with the user account.

I had a look into it, and I am not sure we can support it from Podman.

There are some issues we could circumvent but some others require changes all over the place, including `subgidmap` unless we use only a single map user namespace.

For a test, I've dropped all the `setresgid` both from Podman and crun, so that it always keeps the original ones and the first issue I've seen is that with such configuration we cannot use `newuidmap` or `newgidmap` since they won't allow configuring a process owned by a different gid.  So the first issue is that we must create the pause process using the current method, but then join it without using `setresuid`.

The issue that cannot be circumvented, when you run with a not mapped GID is that you cannot create any file/directory on any file system owned by the rootless user namespace.

If you run with unmapped UID and GID, you can create files on the host, since the unmapped IDs exist there, but you cannot do the same on a file system owned by they rootless user namespace.  This breaks the OCI runtime because we cannot have any nested mounts, which is what we do all the time, e.g. for `/dev/pts` mounted on top of `/dev`.

Here a simpler reproducer:
```
$ id
uid=1000(gscrivano) gid=1000(gscrivano) groups=1000(gscrivano),10(wheel),36(KVM)
$ unshare -r sleep 1000 & 
[2] 1012339
$ newgrp wheel
$ id
uid=1000(gscrivano) gid=10(wheel) groups=10(wheel),36(KVM)
$ nsenter --preserve-credentials -U -t 1012339 unshare -m bash
$ touch /tmp/test-to-create-a-file-on-the-host
$ mount -t tmpfs tmpfs /tmp/
$ touch /tmp/test-to-create-a-file-on-the-tmpfs-i-created
touch: cannot touch '/tmp/test-to-create-a-file-on-the-tmpfs-i-created': Value too large for defined data type
$ 
```

So for a description of what happens above. 

First I create a user namespace with a single ID mapped, the current UID 1000 is mapped to 0, and the same thing happens for the GID.

After, I change the GID to one of the additional groups I am part of.   In this case, I've used the `wheel` group, now I am in your same setup, with my GID different than the user's main GID defined on the system.

With `nsenter` I join the created user namespace and as part of the same command, I create a new mount namespace.

If I create a file on the host `/tmp`, that works fine and it appears as owned by UID=1000 and GID=36 (kvm), as expected.

But the problem arises if I mount a new tmpfs on `/tmp`, that is owned by the current user namespace we are part of.

In this case, any file creation fails because the GID is not known in the user namespace and cannot be mapped back to anything.

There are potentially two ways to get it working, both of them quite complex to implement as well as to maintain.

1) we could configure the user namespace differently and use a single mapping, so your current UID/GID become root in the user namespace and every file is owned by them.  This requires a few changes in Podman and possibly in crun.  It is a half solution though because single mapping is mostly a hack that breaks container images.

2) support multiple mappings is way more difficult.  We'd need to teach Podman/crun to handle a GID different than the user's main GID and try to get the same change in `newgidmap` (which I doubt will be accepted upstream, given it is a privileged program and it must be as tight as possible in what it allows).

As an alternative, the easiest is to join the container manually with `nsenter`:

```
$ podman run --rm -v /tmp:/tmp -d  alpine top
$ podman inspect -l -f '{{.State.Pid}}'
1014863
$ nsenter --preserve-credentials --user --mount --ipc --net --uts -t 1014863 /bin/touch /tmp/test-file
$ ls -l /tmp/test-file 
-rw-r--r--. 1 gscrivano wheel 0 Jun 29 13:17 /tmp/test-file
```

another workaround could be to setup a parent user namespace where you run podman:

```
$ newgrp wheel
$ unshare --map-user=$(id -u) --map-group=$(id -g) bash
$ podman unshare
$ podman run -v /tmp:/tmp --rm alpine touch /tmp/new-file
WARN[0000] Additional gid=1 is not present in the user namespace, skip setting it 
WARN[0000] Additional gid=2 is not present in the user namespace, skip setting it 
WARN[0000] Additional gid=3 is not present in the user namespace, skip setting it 
WARN[0000] Additional gid=4 is not present in the user namespace, skip setting it 
WARN[0000] Additional gid=6 is not present in the user namespace, skip setting it 
WARN[0000] Additional gid=10 is not present in the user namespace, skip setting it 
WARN[0000] Additional gid=11 is not present in the user namespace, skip setting it 
WARN[0000] Additional gid=20 is not present in the user namespace, skip setting it 
WARN[0000] Additional gid=26 is not present in the user namespace, skip setting it 
WARN[0000] Additional gid=27 is not present in the user namespace, skip setting it 
```

then on the host you've:

```
$ ls -nl /tmp/new-file 
-rw-r--r--. 1 1000 10 0 Jun 29 14:36 /tmp/new-file
```

for this to work you need to drop any additional ID from `/etc/subuid` and `/etc/subgid`, add `ignore_chown_errors = "true"` to `storage.conf` and make sure all the podman commands start from the same user namespace you've manually created.
@rknop do the proposed workarounds work for you?

Hi -- the system I'm running this on is not one that I have root on.  (In fact, it's using a modified version of podman: https://github.com/NERSC/podman-hpc .)  It may well be that the solution will need to be somewhere within podman-hpc.  I was hoping, though, that there would be a standard podman way to accomplish what I want.  Anything that requires root access to set up, though, is something I won't be able to do directly.  I will ping the sysadmins here to see if I can get some of the things added to subgid.
